subject: english
title: PSLE English — Synthesis & Transformation Tricks (10 marks)

stats.headline: Synthesis = 10 marks. PSLE rotates the same 6 patterns every year. Learn the keyword-to-pattern map and the most common traps — full marks become straightforward.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Synthesis & Transformation (10 marks)
keyConcepts.body:
**This is a big topic — but we have identified all the patterns and common mistakes.**

Synthesis asks you to REWRITE a sentence using a given keyword, keeping the meaning. The keyword tells you which pattern to use. 6 patterns cover almost every PSLE question in the bank. The hardest part isn't the grammar — it's not changing the meaning, not changing the tense, and not adding new info.
bullets: **A: Linking Words** — Although / Despite / Because / Due to / If / Unless / In order to
**B: Reported Speech** — direct ↔ indirect, with backshift
**C: Pair Words & Preference** — Both/Either/Neither/Not only + Would rather / Prefer
**D: Participle Clauses** — Having + V-ed, Seeing, Upon arriving, Being
**E: Substitution & Inversion** — Instead of / Except / Much to / Little did / No other
**F: Noun Patterns** — Relative (who/whom/which/whose), possessive ('s), verb→noun

**The 3 rules of synthesis:**
① Don't change the meaning. ② Don't change the tense. ③ Don't add info.
Pattern **A: Linking Words** — Although / Despite / Because / Due to / If / Unless / In order to.
Pattern **B: Reported Speech** — direct ↔ indirect, with backshift.
Pattern **C: Pair Words & Preference** — Both/Either/Neither/Not only + Would rather / Prefer.
Pattern **D: Participle Clauses** — Having + V-ed, Seeing, Upon arriving, Being.
Pattern **E: Substitution & Inversion** — Instead of / Except / Much to / Little did / No other.
Pattern **F: Noun Patterns** — Relative (who/whom/which/whose), possessive ('s), verb→noun.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — Linking Words (Although / Because / If / In order to)
keyConcepts.body: Linking words join two sentences into one. They tell the reader the relationship: contrast, cause, condition, or purpose. Each linker has its own grammar rule — clause vs noun phrase. The PSLE trap is mixing them up.
bullets:
**Contrast (Concession)** — two ideas that don't match.
**Although / Even though + CLAUSE**  (subject + verb)
✓ **Although** he was tired, he kept working.
**Despite / In spite of + NOUN PHRASE**
✓ **Despite** his tiredness, he kept working.
✗ Despite he was tired, he kept working.

**Cause** — one thing leads to another.
**Because / Since / As + CLAUSE**
✓ **Because** the bus broke down, we were late.
**Due to / Owing to / On account of + NOUN PHRASE**
✓ **Due to** the bus breakdown, we were late.
✗ Due to the bus broke down, we were late.

**Condition** — if-statements.
**If + present, will + verb**
✓ **If** you hurry, you will catch the bus.
**Unless = if…not** — flip the negative!
✓ **Unless** you hurry, you will miss the bus.
✗ Unless you don't hurry, you will catch the bus.  (double negative)

**Purpose** — why something is done.
**In order to / so as to + verb**  (same subject in both parts)
✓ **In order to** pass the test, Daniel trained daily.
**So that + clause**  (can have a different subject)
✓ Daniel trained daily **so that** he could pass the test.

**The clause-vs-noun conversion trick** (same trick for all four)
Verb → noun (or -ing form), drop the subject.
– "Although it was raining" → "**Despite the rain.**"
– "Because she was sick" → "**Due to her illness.**"
– "If we work hard" → "**In order to succeed.**"

**Common mistakes** (from real student work)
✗ Changing a content word — wrote "homework" when the source said "work". Synthesis is **all-or-nothing**: any swap = 0 marks.
✗ Keeping "not" after **Unless** → double negative ("Unless we do not leave" → just "Unless we leave").
✗ Spelling the derived noun wrong — "indecision" not "indecesion". The noun must be a real word.
✗ Dropping a modifier — "fell ill **suddenly**" → "**sudden** illness", not just "illness". Skipping the adverb costs the mark.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Reported Speech (Direct ↔ Indirect)
keyConcepts.body: Convert direct speech (with quotes) into indirect (no quotes). Headline rule: BACKSHIFT — when the reporting verb is past (said / told / asked), every tense moves one step further back. The biggest trap: past → **past perfect (had + past participle)**.
bullets:
**Tense backshift table** (when reporting verb is past)
– present (am/is/are) → past (was/were)
– present continuous (am eating) → past continuous (was eating)
– **past (broke / went) → past perfect (had broken / had gone)**  ⚠️ the past-past trap
– present perfect (has done) → past perfect (had done)
– will → would   ·   can → could   ·   may → might

**⚠️ The past-past trap** (most-missed)
Direct: "I **broke** the window," John said.
✗ John said that he **broke** the window.
✓ John said that he **had broken** the window.
Past + past = ambiguous time order. Use past perfect for the earlier event.

**Pronoun & time/place shifts**
– I → he/she   ·   you → I/me   ·   we → they   ·   my → his/her
– today → that day   ·   **yesterday → the day before**
– tomorrow → the next day   ·   now → then
– here → there   ·   this → that   ·   these → those

**Questions** — use `asked if / whether`; drop "do/did"; statement order; no question mark.
– "Where do you live?" he asked. → He asked where I **lived**.
– "Are you coming?" she asked. → She asked **if** I **was** coming.

**Commands** — use `told [person] to + verb`.
– "Sit down!" the teacher said. → The teacher **told** us **to sit down**.

**Common mistakes** (from real student work)
✗ Forgetting the verb backshift — "saw" must become "had seen" when the reporting verb is past.
✗ Using vague pronouns when the original spoke to a specific person — meaning shift.
✗ Synonym swaps for time/place words — "the day before" is not the same as "the previous day". Use the EXACT shift words from the table.

keyConcepts.title: Try it — Linking Words & Reported Speech

interactiveQuiz.label: Linking Words — Although → Despite
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Although the weather was bad, the football match continued.
Rewrite using "Despite". Which is correct?
options: Despite the weather was bad, the football match continued.
Despite the bad weather, the football match continued.
Despite the football match, the weather was bad.
Despite of the bad weather, the football match continued.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Despite takes a NOUN PHRASE. "The weather was bad" (clause) becomes "the bad weather". Note: "Despite of" is always wrong — just "Despite".

interactiveQuiz.label: Unless negation trap
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: If you don't wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Rewrite starting with "Unless". Which is correct?
options: Unless you don't wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will not get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will be wet.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Unless" already means "if not", so DROP the "don't" when converting. "If you don't wear" → "Unless you wear". The main clause stays the same — don't add a second negative.

interactiveQuiz.label: Past-past trap
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Direct: "I lost my wallet yesterday," Tom said.
Convert to reported speech. Which is correct?
options: Tom said that he lost his wallet yesterday.
Tom said that he had lost his wallet the day before.
Tom said that he has lost his wallet yesterday.
Tom said that he had lost his wallet yesterday.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Two backshifts must happen together: the verb "lost" (simple past) → "had lost" (past perfect), AND the time word "yesterday" → "the day before". Option 1 misses both. Options 3 and 4 catch one but not the other.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — Pair Words & Preference
keyConcepts.body: Pair words link two equal halves. Preference compares two activities and says which is liked more. Both shapes share one trap: forgetting the right partner word (or the right preposition) on the second half.
bullets:
**Pair Words** — both halves must match in kind (noun + noun, verb + verb).
**Both X and Y**
✓ **Both** Ali **and** Lily like to hike.
**Either X or Y**  /  **Neither X nor Y**
✓ We can **either** play soccer **or** read.
✓ **Neither** Ali **nor** Lily likes swimming.
**As well as** — used inside the sentence to add more info.
✓ Sheila enjoys baking **as well as** her sisters.

**Not only X but (also) Y** — INVERSION after "Not only" at sentence start.
✓ **Not only does** Sarah **sing** beautifully, **but she also** plays the piano well.
✗ "Not only Sarah sings beautifully, but she plays the piano." (no inversion = wrong)

**The "Not only" inversion formula** (must be memorised):
**Not only + DOES / DID / HAS + Subject + BASE VERB**
✓ Not only **does** Sarah **sing** beautifully…
✓ Not only **did** Ali **win** the race…
✓ Not only **has** Mark **finished** his work…
Notice: the verb after the subject is in BASE form (sing, win, finished). The tense is carried by the helper (does, did, has).

**X or Y** (warning + consequence form):
✓ Hand in your assignment on time **or** your marks will be deducted.

**Preference — three forms** tested most:
**Would rather + bare verb + than + bare verb**
✓ Alice would rather **read** than **watch TV**.
✗ Alice would rather **read** than **to watch TV**.   (no "to")
**Prefer + V-ing to V-ing**  /  **Prefer + noun to noun**
✓ My grandparents prefer **visiting** local attractions **to travelling** overseas.
**Have a preference for X over Y**
✓ My grandparents have a **preference for** visiting local attractions **over** travelling overseas.
✗ "preference to travelling" — wrong preposition.

**Common mistakes**
✗ Forgetting the inversion after "Not only" — costs all the marks.
✗ Using gerunds after "would rather" — it takes BARE verbs.
✗ Using "to" after "than" — "would rather X than Y", never "than to".
✗ "preference to" — the correct preposition is "preference **for**…**over**".

keyConcepts.title: Pattern D — Participle Clauses (Having / -ing / Upon)
keyConcepts.body: Participle clauses compress two sentences into one by replacing the first sentence with a participle (Having + V-ed or just V-ing). The new subject moves into the second clause. Same trick three flavours.
bullets:
**Having + past participle, + new subject + verb**  (action 1 finished, then action 2)
✓ Mary **finished** her homework. She went out to play.
→ **Having finished** her homework, **Mary** went out to play.

**Not having** (negative form)
✓ Amy **had not baked** cookies before. She burnt all of them.
→ **Not having baked** cookies before, **Amy** burnt all of them.

**Bare -ing participle** (verb that describes what someone is doing or seeing/hearing when something else happens)
✓ The boy **heard** a loud noise. He quickly looked up.
→ **Hearing** a loud noise, the boy quickly looked up.
✓ Mary **saw** her friend. She waved.
→ **Seeing** her friend, Mary waved.

**Upon / On / After / Before + V-ing**  (time linkers)
✓ **Upon arriving** at the station, we boarded the train.
✓ **After finishing** dinner, John washed the dishes.
✓ **Before leaving** the house, lock the door.

**Being + adjective / past participle**  (state of being, often passive)
✓ **Being too shy**, Hamid kept quiet.
✓ **Being late**, she missed the bus.

**Three checkpoints** before submitting:
① Past participle is correct (drew → **drawn**, eat → **eaten**, write → **written**).
② **his / her / their** possessives match the original — DON'T change them.
③ The new subject is in the SECOND clause, not repeated in the first.

**Common mistakes** (from real student work)
✗ "Having **drew**" — should be "Having **drawn**". The past participle of "draw" is "drawn".
✗ Changing "his / her" to "a" or "the" — meaning shift = 0 marks.
✗ Repeating the subject in both clauses ("Having finished her homework, **she** Mary went…"). Pick one — usually the proper noun in the second clause.

interactiveQuiz.label: Pair Words — Not only
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Sarah sings beautifully. She plays the piano well.
Rewrite starting with "Not only". Which is correct?
options: Not only Sarah sings beautifully, but she also plays the piano well.
Not only does Sarah sings beautifully, but she also plays the piano well.
Not only does Sarah sing beautifully, but she also plays the piano well.
Sarah not only sings beautifully and she plays the piano well.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Not only" at the start FORCES inversion: "does + Sarah + BASE verb". Option 1 has no inversion; option 2 keeps "sings" (after "does" the verb must be in base form); option 4 rearranges and drops "but also". Option 3 inverts correctly and keeps both halves balanced with "but she also".

interactiveQuiz.label: Preference — would rather
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Rani enjoys cycling. She does not enjoy swimming.
Rewrite using "would rather". Which is correct?
options: Rani would rather cycle than to swim.
Rani would rather cycling than swimming.
Rani would rather cycle than swim.
Rani prefers cycle than swim.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Would rather" is followed by the BARE infinitive (no "to" and no -ing). Both halves use the same form — cycle / swim. Option 1 adds "to" by mistake; option 2 uses gerunds; option 4 confuses two patterns.

interactiveQuiz.label: Participle — Having
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: John drew a picture of his dog. Then he framed it up.
Rewrite starting with "Having". Which is correct?
options: Having drew a picture of his dog, John framed it up.
Having drawn a picture of a dog, John framed it up.
Having drawn a picture of his dog, John framed it up.
John drew a picture of his dog, having framed it up.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Two checks. Verb form: "drawn" (past participle), not "drew" — that rules out option 1. Possessive: "his dog" stays "his dog"; option 2 changed it to "a" which changes the meaning. Option 4 puts "having framed" on the wrong action.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern E — Substitution & Inversion
keyConcepts.body: A small but reliable family. Substitution swaps one action for another ("Instead of") or removes one item from a group ("Except"). Inversion flips the normal subject-verb order after a negative starter ("Little did he know…"). PSLE loves these because the starter word forces a specific structure.
bullets:
**Instead of + V-ing** (or noun) — replaces one action with another.
✓ Ahmad did not complete his homework. He watched TV.
→ **Instead of** completing his homework, Ahmad watched TV.

**Except / except for + noun** — removes one item from a group.
✓ All the children were pleased with their gifts. Linda was not pleased.
→ All the children were pleased with their gifts **except** Linda.

**Much to + possessive + noun** — describes a reaction (surprise, delight, disappointment).
✓ Gavin was very pleased to receive the present.
→ **Much to** Gavin's delight, he received the present.
✓ The team was disappointed when the match was called off.
→ **Much to** the team's disappointment, the match was called off.

**Negative Inversion** — when a NEGATIVE word starts the sentence, the verb INVERTS.
**Little did + Subject + base verb**
✓ Ben never knew he would top the class.
→ **Little did Ben know** he would top the class.
**No other + noun + verb**
✓ Rita can perform magic tricks. No one else can.
→ **No other student can** perform magic tricks the way Rita does.
**No sooner + had + S + V-ed + than…**
✓ As soon as the bell rang, the students left.
→ **No sooner had** the bell rung **than** the students left.

**The inversion rule** (must be memorised):
After a negative starter (Little / No other / No sooner / Hardly / Scarcely / Never),
flip Subject + Verb → Helper (does/did/has/had) + Subject + Base Verb.
Same pattern as "Not only" inversion.

**Common mistakes**
✗ Forgetting inversion after "Little did" — "Little did he knew" (should be "Little did he **know**" with base verb).
✗ Using "Instead of + clause" — should be **Instead of + V-ing** (or noun). "Instead of he watched TV" → "Instead of watching TV".
✗ Confusing "Except" (excludes one) with "Besides" (includes more). Different meanings.
✗ Possessive after "Much to" — "Gavin's delight", not "Gavin delight" (the 's matters).

keyConcepts.title: Pattern F — Noun Patterns (Relative / Possessive / Verb→Noun)
keyConcepts.body: Noun patterns reshape information about a noun. Three flavours: relative clauses describe a noun ("the student **who** won"), possessives compress a sentence into a noun phrase ("**Sally's** insincerity annoyed them"), and verb-to-noun conversions change an action into a noun ("the **loss** of her phone"). All three squeeze a clause into a noun-shaped piece.
bullets:
**Relative pronouns — who / whom / which / whose**
– **who** = subject (people). Replace with he/she/they.
– **whom** = object (people). Replace with him/her/them.
– **which** = things.
– **whose** = possessive (people or things).
✓ My mother bought a new blender **which can** make fruit juices.
✓ The boy **whose** dog was lost is my classmate.

**POSITION RULE — the relative pronoun must sit RIGHT AFTER the noun it describes.**
Original: We admire Mr Kwan. He is our local football player.
✓ **Mr Kwan, whom we admire,** is our local football player.
✗ Mr Kwan is our local football player, **whom we admire**.   (whom is detached from Mr Kwan)

**Defining vs non-defining clauses — the comma rule:**
– **Defining clause = essential info, NO commas.** The clause picks WHICH one you mean.
✓ The robber **who wore a black mask** was caught.
– **Non-defining clause = extra info, USES commas.** The clause adds detail you could remove.
✓ Mr Kwan, **whom we admire**, is our local football player.
Quick test: remove the clause. If the meaning still holds → non-defining, use commas.

**Possessive transformation** — compress "X was [adjective]" or "X did Y" into an 's noun phrase.
✓ Sally was insincere. They were annoyed.
→ **Sally's** insincerity annoyed them.
✓ The boy worked hard. His effort impressed his teacher.
→ The **boy's** hard work impressed his teacher.
✓ If Mr Ahmad had not advised us…
→ **Without Mr Ahmad's** advice…

**Verb → noun conversion** — change the action into a noun. PSLE drills this every year.
✓ Vasanthi **lost** her phone. She reported it.
→ Vasanthi reported **the loss** of her phone.
✓ The diver **adjusted** his goggles slightly.
→ The diver **made slight adjustments** to his goggles.
✓ The meeting **concluded** in the evening.
→ The meeting **came to a conclusion** in the evening.
Common openers: "made" + noun, "came" + to / for + noun, "took" + noun, "had" + a + noun.

**Common mistakes** (from real student work)
✗ Placing the relative clause far from the noun — synthesis is **all-or-nothing**: position wrong = 0 marks.
✗ "whom is" — always wrong. The subject pronoun is "who".
✗ Wrong noun form — "indecesion" instead of "indecision", "concluding" instead of "conclusion". The noun must be a real word and spelled correctly.
✗ Dropping a modifier when converting verb → noun. "adjusted slightly" → "**slight** adjustments", not just "adjustments".

interactiveQuiz.label: Substitution — Instead of
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Ahmad did not complete his homework. He watched TV.
Rewrite using "Instead of". Which is correct?
options: Instead of he watched TV, Ahmad did not complete his homework.
Instead of completing his homework, Ahmad watched TV.
Instead of his homework, Ahmad completed TV.
Instead of complete his homework, Ahmad watched TV.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Instead of" takes the -ing form (or a noun), not a clause and not a bare verb. "Did not complete his homework" turns into "completing his homework". Option 4 uses the bare verb "complete" — also wrong.

interactiveQuiz.label: Inversion — Little did
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Ben never knew that he would top the class.
Rewrite starting with "Little did". Which is correct?
options: Little did Ben knew that he would top the class.
Little Ben did know that he would top the class.
Little did Ben know that he would top the class.
Little did know Ben that he would top the class.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Negative starter forces inversion: Little did + Subject + BASE verb. The base form of "knew" is "know". Option 1 keeps "knew" (wrong). Option 2 has no inversion. Option 4 puts the subject in the wrong slot.

interactiveQuiz.label: Verb → Noun
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: The meeting concluded only in the evening.
Rewrite starting with "The meeting came". Which is correct?
options: The meeting came to a conclusion only in the evening.
The meeting came concluded only in the evening.
The meeting came conclusion only in the evening.
The meeting came to conclude only in the evening.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: The verb "concluded" must change into the noun "conclusion". The fixed phrase is **came to a conclusion** — needs "to a" before the noun. Options 2–4 either keep the verb form or drop "a".

keyConcepts.title: Start practising!
keyConcepts.body: Mixed practice drawn from PSLE 2014-2024 Synthesis & Transformation. Covers all 6 patterns from the slides. Trains the reflex of checking "same tense, same info, same meaning" on every rewrite.
bullets: **24 questions** — **4 from each of the 6 patterns**
Wrong answers auto-saved, reviewed in 1 week
AI marks word-for-word — partial credit if you almost got it but missed tense/info
You'll tackle **24 questions** — exactly **4 from each of the 6 patterns** in this class.
Any Wrong answers are auto-saved, and reviewed in 1 week.
The AI marks your answers word-for-word — giving partial credit if you almost got it but missed the tense/info.

keyWords.definition: Two contrasting ideas joined: 'tired BUT kept going'. Keywords: Although + clause / Despite + noun phrase / In spite of + noun phrase. The grammar split (clause vs noun phrase) is the most common trap.
Same grammar split as concession. Because/Since/As take a clause (subject + verb). Due to / Owing to take a noun phrase. Convert verb → noun + drop subject.
Means 'if...not'. When converting If → Unless, NEGATE the if-clause (drop 'not' or add 'not'). Double negatives are the #1 trap.
Purpose linker. Used to explain WHY an action is taken. 'In order to + verb' (same subject in both halves) or 'So that + clause' (can have a different subject).

In reported speech, when the reporting verb is past, every tense shifts one step further back: present → past, past → past perfect (had + past participle). Pronouns and time/place words shift too: yesterday → the day before, here → there.
Compares two activities. Followed by BARE verbs: 'would rather X than Y' (no 'to' before Y). 'Prefer' uses gerunds: 'prefer V-ing to V-ing'. 'Preference' uses 'for X over Y'.
When 'Not only' starts the sentence, the verb INVERTS: 'Not only DOES Sarah sing beautifully, but she also plays the piano well'. Forgetting the 'does / did / has' costs 1 mark.
After a negative starter (Little / No other / No sooner / Hardly / Scarcely), subject-verb order flips. Pattern: starter + helper (does/did/has/had) + subject + BASE verb. Same rule as Not only.

Joins two actions where the first finishes before the second starts. Replaces 'Subject + did X. Then subject did Y' with 'Having + V-ed X, subject did Y'. Possessives in the original (his / her / their) must stay unchanged.
Same idea as 'Having' but for ongoing actions or sensing. 'Hearing a loud noise, the boy looked up.' Use the bare V-ing form at the start; new subject comes in the second clause.
Substitution. Replaces one action with another. Must use -ing form (or a noun), NEVER a clause or bare verb. 'Instead of completing his homework, Ahmad watched TV.'
Describes a reaction. 'Much to Gavin's delight…' / 'Much to the team's disappointment…'. The 's possessive is essential — 'Gavin delight' is wrong.
who = subject (replaces he/she/they) — 'who teaches us'. whom = object (replaces him/her/them) — 'whom we admire'. Test: if you could substitute 'he/she' use 'who'; if 'him/her' use 'whom'.

Change an action verb into a noun phrase. 'lost her phone' → 'the loss of her phone'. 'adjusted slightly' → 'made slight adjustments'. 'concluded' → 'came to a conclusion'. Common openers: made / came to / took / had a.
① Don't change the tense. ② Don't add info. ③ Don't change the meaning. Every rewrite must pass all three checks before you submit.

subTopics.label: Reported Speech (Direct ↔ Indirect)
subTopics.description: Direct ↔ indirect. Backshift tenses one step (present → past, past → past perfect). Also shift pronouns and time words (yesterday → the day before). Past-past trap is the headline mistake.

subTopics.label: Linking Words (Although / Because / If / In order to)
subTopics.description: Combines concession (Although/Despite/No matter/However/But/While), cause (Because/Since/As/Due to/Owing to/So), condition (If/Unless/Provided/As long as) and purpose (In order to/So that). All share the clause-vs-noun-phrase conversion trick.

subTopics.label: Pair Words & Preference
subTopics.description: Pair words (Both/Either/Neither/Not only/As well as/Or) plus preference (Would rather/Prefer/Preference for). Headline trap: 'Not only' at sentence start FORCES inversion ('Not only DOES Sarah sing'). 'Would rather' takes bare verbs.

subTopics.label: Participle Clauses (Having / -ing)
subTopics.description: Joins two actions by reducing the first clause into a participle. Forms: Having + V-ed (sequential), bare V-ing (concurrent — Seeing/Hearing), Upon/After/Before + V-ing (temporal), and Being + adjective (state).

subTopics.label: Substitution & Inversion
subTopics.description: Substitution (Instead of + V-ing, Except + noun, Much to + possessive). Inversion after a negative starter (Little did / No other / No sooner / Hardly): negative + helper + Subject + BASE verb. Same flip as 'Not only'.

subTopics.label: Noun Patterns (Relative / Possessive / Verb→Noun)
subTopics.description: Reshapes information about a noun. Relative clauses (who/whom/which/whose), possessive 's transformations (Sally's insincerity), and verb→noun conversions ('made slight adjustments', 'came to a conclusion'). The position rule and exact-spelling rule cost marks if missed.
